nodes.label: African Roots
nodes.summary: The foundational musical traditions of Africa and the African diaspora — spirituals, work songs, and field hollers that seeded blues, jazz, gospel, and virtually all popular music.

nodes.label: Classical Western
nodes.summary: The European art music tradition spanning from medieval plainchant through Baroque, Classical, Romantic, and into 20th-century modernism and minimalism.

nodes.label: Rock
nodes.summary: Born from blues, gospel, and country in the 1950s, rock music became the dominant popular music form of the 20th century, branching into dozens of subgenres.

nodes.label: Electronic
nodes.summary: Music primarily produced with electronic instruments, synthesizers, drum machines, and digital audio workstations — from early experiments to global dance culture.

nodes.label: Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: A cultural movement and music genre born in the Bronx in 1973, built on DJing, MCing, breakdancing, and graffiti — now the world's most consumed music genre.

nodes.label: Jazz & Blues
nodes.summary: The twin pillars of African-American music — blues rooted in field hollers and spirituals, jazz in improvisation and harmonic complexity. Together they shaped the 20th century.

nodes.label: Latin
nodes.summary: The rich musical traditions of Latin America and the Caribbean — from Cuban son and Brazilian samba to Argentine tango and modern reggaeton.

nodes.label: World
nodes.summary: Musical traditions from Jamaica, East Asia, South Asia, and Iberia that cross-pollinated with Western popular music to create globally influential genres.

nodes.label: West African Music
nodes.summary: The polyrhythmic, call-and-response vocal traditions of West Africa — griot storytelling, drumming circles, and pentatonic scales that enslaved Africans carried to the Americas.

nodes.label: Spirituals
nodes.summary: Sacred songs created by enslaved African Americans, blending African musical traditions with Christian hymns. Coded messages of resistance and hope that laid groundwork for gospel and blues.

nodes.label: Work Songs
nodes.summary: Rhythmic songs sung by enslaved and later free Black laborers to coordinate physical work. Their call-and-response structure and blue notes fed directly into the blues.

nodes.label: Field Hollers
nodes.summary: Solo vocal cries sung by enslaved field workers in the American South. Their melismatic, improvised melodies and bent notes are direct precursors to the blues vocal style.

nodes.label: Afrobeat
nodes.summary: Genre pioneered by Fela Kuti in Lagos in the late 1960s, fusing Yoruba music and Ghanaian highlife with American funk, jazz, and psychedelic rock. Politically charged and rhythmically complex.

nodes.label: Highlife
nodes.summary: A Ghanaian and Nigerian popular music genre from the early 20th century, blending local melodies with Western instruments — brass bands, guitars, and jazz harmonies. A key ingredient of Afrobeat.

nodes.label: Blues
nodes.summary: Born in the Deep South around the 1890s-1900s from spirituals, work songs, and field hollers. The 12-bar blues form, blue notes, and call-and-response became the DNA of popular music.

nodes.label: Delta Blues
nodes.summary: The earliest recorded blues style, originating in the Mississippi Delta in the 1900s-1920s. Acoustic guitar and slide guitar, raw vocals. Robert Johnson, Charley Patton, Son House.

nodes.label: Chicago Blues
nodes.summary: Electrified Delta blues transplanted to Chicago during the Great Migration (1940s-1950s). Muddy Waters, Howlin Wolf, and Little Walter added electric guitar, amplified harmonica, and full bands.

nodes.label: Rhythm and Blues
nodes.summary: Emerged in the late 1940s as a commercial term for African-American popular music. Combined blues, jazz, and gospel into an upbeat, dance-oriented style that directly birthed rock and roll.

nodes.label: Jazz
nodes.summary: Originated in New Orleans in the 1910s from blues, ragtime, and brass band traditions. Defined by improvisation, swing, and complex harmony. America's classical music.

nodes.label: Swing
nodes.summary: The dominant popular music of the 1930s-1940s. Big band jazz with danceable rhythms — Duke Ellington, Count Basie, Benny Goodman. The first truly mass-market American music.

nodes.label: Bebop
nodes.summary: A virtuosic, harmonically complex jazz style developed in the mid-1940s by Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, and Thelonious Monk. Fast tempos, intricate melodies, and improvisation over chord changes.

nodes.label: Cool Jazz
nodes.summary: A relaxed, lyrical response to bebop's intensity, emerging in the late 1940s-1950s. Miles Davis's Birth of the Cool (1957), Dave Brubeck, Chet Baker. West Coast jazz.

nodes.label: Hard Bop
nodes.summary: A grittier, blues- and gospel-inflected extension of bebop, flourishing in the 1950s-1960s. Art Blakey, Horace Silver, Cannonball Adderley. The East Coast response to cool jazz.

nodes.label: Free Jazz
nodes.summary: Avant-garde jazz that abandoned fixed chord progressions, tempos, and structures in the 1960s. Ornette Coleman, John Coltrane, Cecil Taylor. Radical freedom as aesthetic principle.

nodes.label: Jazz Fusion
nodes.summary: Jazz fused with rock, funk, and electronic instruments in the late 1960s-1970s. Miles Davis's Bitches Brew (1970), Weather Report, Herbie Hancock, Return to Forever.

nodes.label: Smooth Jazz
nodes.summary: A commercially oriented, radio-friendly jazz style emphasizing melody over improvisation. Emerged in the 1980s from fusion. Grover Washington Jr., Kenny G, George Benson.

nodes.label: Gospel
nodes.summary: African-American sacred music rooted in spirituals, hymns, and the Black church. Thomas A. Dorsey formalized it in the 1930s. Its vocal intensity and emotional power shaped soul, R&B, and rock.

nodes.label: Soul
nodes.summary: Born in the late 1950s-1960s by fusing gospel fervor with R&B rhythm. Ray Charles, Sam Cooke, Aretha Franklin, Otis Redding. Motown and Stax defined two distinct approaches.

nodes.label: Funk
nodes.summary: James Brown and Parliament-Funkadelic made the groove the song itself in the late 1960s-1970s. Syncopated bass, rhythmic guitar, heavy drums. The One. Directly seeded hip-hop and electronic dance music.

nodes.label: Rock and Roll
nodes.summary: Exploded in the mid-1950s from rhythm and blues, gospel, and country. Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Elvis Presley, Fats Domino. The cultural earthquake that launched the modern music era.

nodes.label: British Invasion
nodes.summary: UK bands reinterpreted American blues and rock and roll for global audiences in the early-mid 1960s. The Beatles, Rolling Stones, The Who, The Kinks. Reshaped popular music worldwide.

nodes.label: Psychedelic Rock
nodes.summary: Mid-to-late 1960s rock exploring altered consciousness through studio effects, Eastern instruments, and extended improvisation. Jimi Hendrix, Pink Floyd, Jefferson Airplane, The Doors.

nodes.label: Progressive Rock
nodes.summary: Late 1960s-1970s rock that incorporated classical structures, odd time signatures, concept albums, and virtuoso musicianship. Yes, Genesis, King Crimson, Pink Floyd, ELP.

nodes.label: Hard Rock
nodes.summary: Aggressive, amplified blues-rock with distorted guitars and powerful vocals, emerging in the late 1960s. Led Zeppelin, Deep Purple, AC/DC. The bridge between blues-rock and heavy metal.

nodes.label: Punk Rock
nodes.summary: A deliberate rejection of progressive rock's complexity, erupting in 1976-77. Ramones, Sex Pistols, The Clash. Fast, loud, stripped-down, DIY. Three chords and the truth.

nodes.label: Post-Punk
nodes.summary: Late 1970s-early 1980s movement that retained punk's energy but embraced experimentation — synthesizers, art, angular guitars. Joy Division, Siouxsie, Wire, Talking Heads.

nodes.label: New Wave
nodes.summary: Pop-oriented post-punk with synthesizers, quirky aesthetics, and mainstream appeal (late 1970s-1980s). Blondie, Devo, The Cars, Duran Duran, Depeche Mode.

nodes.label: Heavy Metal
nodes.summary: Born from hard rock circa 1970 with Black Sabbath's debut. Distorted guitars, powerful drums, dark themes. Spawned dozens of subgenres over five decades.

nodes.label: Thrash Metal
nodes.summary: Fast, aggressive metal fusing NWOBHM with hardcore punk in the early 1980s. Metallica, Slayer, Megadeth, Anthrax — the Big Four. Speed, precision, and socio-political anger.

nodes.label: Death Metal
nodes.summary: Extreme metal pushing thrash to its limits in the mid-late 1980s. Growled vocals, blast beats, technical riffing. Death, Morbid Angel, Cannibal Corpse. Florida and Sweden scenes.

nodes.label: Black Metal
nodes.summary: Extreme metal emphasizing atmosphere, tremolo picking, shrieked vocals, and anti-establishment ideology. Emerged in the 1980s-1990s. Bathory, Mayhem, Burzum. Norwegian scene notorious.

nodes.label: Alternative Rock
nodes.summary: An umbrella term for underground rock emerging from the 1980s indie and post-punk scenes, breaking mainstream with Nirvana in 1991. R.E.M., Pixies, Radiohead, Smashing Pumpkins.

nodes.label: Grunge
nodes.summary: Seattle sound fusing punk energy with heavy metal riffs and introspective lyrics (mid-1980s-1990s). Nirvana, Pearl Jam, Soundgarden, Alice in Chains. Killed hair metal, defined Generation X.

nodes.label: Indie Rock
nodes.summary: Rock released on independent labels or with a DIY ethos, flourishing from the 1980s onward. Sonic Youth, Pavement, Modest Mouse, Arcade Fire. Aesthetic independence over commercial appeal.

nodes.label: Shoegaze
nodes.summary: Late 1980s-early 1990s UK genre defined by walls of distorted guitar, ethereal vocals, and heavy effects pedal use. My Bloody Valentine, Slowdive, Ride. Named for performers staring at their pedals.

nodes.label: Garage Rock
nodes.summary: Raw, energetic rock played by amateur bands in the 1960s — The Sonics, The Kingsmen, 13th Floor Elevators. Proto-punk spirit. Revived in the 2000s by The Strokes and White Stripes.

nodes.label: Surf Rock
nodes.summary: Early 1960s Southern California genre featuring reverb-drenched guitar instrumentals and beach culture themes. Dick Dale, The Beach Boys, The Ventures. Reverb as identity.

nodes.label: Folk Rock
nodes.summary: Mid-1960s fusion of folk songwriting and rock instrumentation. Bob Dylan going electric (1965), The Byrds, Simon & Garfunkel. Protest lyrics met amplified sound.

nodes.label: Country Rock
nodes.summary: Late 1960s-1970s blend of rock with country and western elements. The Byrds (Sweetheart of the Rodeo), Gram Parsons, Eagles, Creedence Clearwater Revival.

nodes.label: Electronic Music
nodes.summary: Music produced primarily through electronic means — from early tape experiments and Moog synthesizers in the 1950s-60s to the global dance music industry.

nodes.label: Synthesizer Music
nodes.summary: Music centered on the synthesizer, from Wendy Carlos's Switched-On Bach (1968) to Kraftwerk's electronic pop (1970s). The DX7 and TR-808 revolutionized production in the 1980s.

nodes.label: Disco
nodes.summary: 1970s dance music born in underground clubs of New York — funk, soul, and Latin rhythms with four-on-the-floor beats. Donna Summer, Bee Gees, Giorgio Moroder. Catalyzed house and techno.

nodes.label: House
nodes.summary: Born in Chicago clubs in the early 1980s from disco, electronic pop, and drum machines. Frankie Knuckles, the Warehouse. Four-on-the-floor kick, synthesized basslines, soulful vocals.

nodes.label: Techno
nodes.summary: Emerged in Detroit in the mid-1980s, created by Juan Atkins, Derrick May, and Kevin Saunderson. Fused Chicago house, Kraftwerk's electronic sound, and Detroit funk into futuristic machine music.

nodes.label: Trance
nodes.summary: Melodic, hypnotic electronic dance music that emerged in Germany and the Netherlands in the early 1990s. Build-ups, breakdowns, and euphoric synth leads. Paul van Dyk, Tiesto, Armin van Buuren.

nodes.label: Drum and Bass
nodes.summary: Fast-tempo (160-180 BPM) UK electronic genre from the early 1990s, emphasizing breakbeats and heavy bass. Evolved from rave and jungle. Goldie, Roni Size, LTJ Bukem.

nodes.label: Ambient
nodes.summary: Music emphasizing atmosphere, tone, and texture over rhythm or melody. Brian Eno coined the term with Ambient 1: Music for Airports (1978). Aphex Twin, Stars of the Lid.

nodes.label: IDM
nodes.summary: Intelligent dance music — experimental electronic music for listening rather than dancing, emerging in the early 1990s. Aphex Twin, Autechre, Boards of Canada. Warp Records.

nodes.label: Dubstep
nodes.summary: Born in South London in the early 2000s from UK garage, dub, and drum and bass. Deep sub-bass, sparse rhythms, half-time beats. Skream, Burial, Benga. Later popularized by Skrillex.

nodes.label: EDM
nodes.summary: Umbrella term for festival-oriented electronic dance music that dominated the 2010s. Big-room drops, pop crossovers, Las Vegas residencies. Avicii, Calvin Harris, Marshmello.

nodes.label: Synthwave
nodes.summary: 2000s-2010s retro-electronic genre inspired by 1980s film soundtracks, video games, and synth-pop. Analog synths, neon aesthetics. Kavinsky, Perturbator, Carpenter Brut.

nodes.label: Vaporwave
nodes.summary: Internet-born micro-genre from the early 2010s — slowed-down samples of 1980s-90s muzak, smooth jazz, and pop. Critique of consumer capitalism through surreal nostalgia. Macintosh Plus.

nodes.label: Lo-Fi
nodes.summary: Chill, low-fidelity hip-hop-influenced beats popularized on YouTube and streaming platforms in the 2010s. Jazz samples, vinyl crackle, relaxed tempos. Nujabes as spiritual predecessor.

nodes.label: Industrial
nodes.summary: Abrasive, confrontational electronic music using noise, distortion, and mechanical sounds. Throbbing Gristle (1976), Einsturzende Neubauten, Nine Inch Nails, Ministry.

nodes.label: Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: Born August 11, 1973, at a Bronx block party. DJ Kool Herc isolated funk breakbeats, MCs rapped over them. Jamaican sound system culture meets African-American street culture.

nodes.label: Old School Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: The earliest recorded hip-hop era (1979-1984). Simple beats, party-oriented rapping, DJ scratching. Sugarhill Gang, Grandmaster Flash, Afrika Bambaataa, Run-DMC.

nodes.label: Golden Age Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: The creative peak of hip-hop (late 1980s-early 1990s). Diverse styles, innovative sampling, lyrical sophistication. Rakim, Public Enemy, A Tribe Called Quest, De La Soul, Nas.

nodes.label: Gangsta Rap
nodes.summary: Subgenre depicting street life, violence, and systemic injustice. Emerged in the late 1980s. N.W.A, Ice-T, Tupac, Notorious B.I.G. Controversial but culturally transformative.

nodes.label: East Coast Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: New York-centered hip-hop emphasizing lyrical complexity, jazz-influenced production, and boom-bap beats. Nas, Wu-Tang Clan, Notorious B.I.G., Jay-Z.

nodes.label: West Coast Hip-Hop
nodes.summary: Los Angeles-centered hip-hop with G-funk production, synthesizer-heavy beats, and laid-back flows. Dr. Dre, Snoop Dogg, Tupac, Ice Cube. The Chronic (1992) defined the sound.

nodes.label: Southern Rap
nodes.summary: Hip-hop from the American South — Atlanta, Houston, Memphis, New Orleans. Distinct bass-heavy production, regional slang, crunk energy. OutKast, UGK, Three 6 Mafia, Lil Wayne.

nodes.label: Trap
nodes.summary: Atlanta-born subgenre defined by 808 bass, hi-hat rolls, and dark synthesizers. Named after T.I.'s Trap Muzik (2003), popularized by Lex Luger and Waka Flocka in 2010. Dominated 2010s hip-hop.

nodes.label: Conscious Rap
nodes.summary: Socially and politically aware hip-hop focusing on knowledge, justice, and Black empowerment. KRS-One, Common, Talib Kweli, Kendrick Lamar. The thinking person's rap.

nodes.label: Drill
nodes.summary: Aggressive hip-hop subgenre born in Chicago (early 2010s) from trap music and gangsta rap. Chief Keef, Lil Durk, Young Chop. Spread to London (UK drill) and Brooklyn.

nodes.label: Medieval Music
nodes.summary: European music from roughly 500-1400 CE. Gregorian chant, troubadour songs, early polyphony. Sacred and secular traditions before notation standardized. Hildegard von Bingen.

nodes.label: Baroque
nodes.summary: Ornate, dramatic European art music (1600-1750). Tonal harmony, basso continuo, fugue, concerto grosso. Bach, Handel, Vivaldi. The foundation of Western harmonic language.

nodes.label: Classical Period
nodes.summary: The age of elegance, balance, and form (1750-1820). Sonata form, symphony, string quartet. Haydn, Mozart, early Beethoven. Clarity and emotional restraint.

nodes.label: Romantic
nodes.summary: Emotional intensity, expanded orchestras, nationalism, and individual expression (1820-1900). Beethoven, Chopin, Wagner, Tchaikovsky, Brahms. Art as feeling.

nodes.label: Impressionism
nodes.summary: Late 19th-early 20th century movement emphasizing atmosphere, color, and mood over structure. Debussy, Ravel. Parallel fifths, whole-tone scales, shimmering orchestration.

nodes.label: Modernist
nodes.summary: Early-mid 20th century break with Romantic tradition. Atonality, serialism, neoclassicism, primitivism. Schoenberg, Stravinsky, Bartok. Radical reinvention of what music could be.

nodes.label: Minimalism
nodes.summary: Repetitive structures, gradual transformation, and consonant harmony — a reaction against serialist complexity in the 1960s-70s. Steve Reich, Philip Glass, Terry Riley, La Monte Young.

nodes.label: Contemporary Classical
nodes.summary: Art music from the late 20th century onward — spectral music, post-minimalism, electroacoustic, new complexity. John Adams, Kaija Saariaho, Thomas Ades. Pluralistic and eclectic.

nodes.label: Opera
nodes.summary: Dramatic vocal art form combining music, theater, and spectacle, born in Florence around 1600. Monteverdi, Mozart, Verdi, Wagner, Puccini. The total work of art.

nodes.label: Chamber Music
nodes.summary: Intimate ensemble music for small groups without conductor — string quartets, piano trios, sonatas. Haydn, Beethoven, Schubert, Bartok. The art of musical conversation.

nodes.label: Salsa
nodes.summary: Dance music forged in 1960s-1970s New York by Cuban and Puerto Rican immigrants, blending son cubano, mambo, and jazz. Celia Cruz, Hector Lavoe, Willie Colon. Fania Records.

nodes.label: Bossa Nova
nodes.summary: Quiet revolution in Brazilian music (late 1950s). Antonio Carlos Jobim and Joao Gilberto fused samba rhythm with jazz harmony. The Girl from Ipanema became a global standard.

nodes.label: Samba
nodes.summary: Afro-Brazilian music and dance rooted in West African rhythms, born in Rio de Janeiro in the early 20th century. Syncopated percussion, Carnival parades, batucada. Brazil's national sound.

nodes.label: Cumbia
nodes.summary: Colombian folk genre blending Indigenous, African, and European elements, originating on the Caribbean coast. Spread across Latin America, evolving locally in Mexico, Argentina, and Peru.

nodes.label: Reggaeton
nodes.summary: Born from reggae en espanol in Panama (late 1980s) and Puerto Rican underground, fusing dancehall reggae with hip-hop. Dembow beat. Daddy Yankee, Bad Bunny. Now dominates global pop.

nodes.label: Tango
nodes.summary: Argentine music and dance born in the working-class neighborhoods of Buenos Aires in the late 19th century. Bandoneon, passionate expression. Carlos Gardel, Astor Piazzolla (nuevo tango).

nodes.label: Mariachi
nodes.summary: Traditional Mexican folk ensemble music from Jalisco, featuring violins, trumpets, guitars, and vihuela. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Ranchera singing and son jalisciense roots.

nodes.label: Latin Pop
nodes.summary: Spanish-language pop music blending Latin rhythms with mainstream pop production. Crossover appeal from Gloria Estefan and Ricky Martin to Shakira and Bad Bunny.

nodes.label: Reggae
nodes.summary: Jamaican music emerging in the late 1960s from ska and rocksteady. Off-beat rhythmic guitar, bass-heavy grooves, Rastafari spirituality. Bob Marley made it a global language of resistance.

nodes.label: Ska
nodes.summary: Jamaican genre from the late 1950s combining Caribbean mento and calypso with American R&B and jazz. Uptempo, walking bass, off-beat guitar. Precursor to rocksteady and reggae.

nodes.label: Dub
nodes.summary: Jamaican production style (early 1970s) stripping reggae to drums and bass, adding echo, reverb, and delay. King Tubby, Lee Scratch Perry. The birth of remix culture and producer as artist.

nodes.label: K-Pop
nodes.summary: South Korean popular music blending pop, hip-hop, R&B, and electronic dance music with synchronized choreography and visual spectacle. BTS, BLACKPINK. Global phenomenon since the 2010s.

nodes.label: J-Pop
nodes.summary: Japanese popular music encompassing a wide range of styles from the 1990s onward. Influenced by Western rock, electronic, and pop but with distinctly Japanese aesthetics and idol culture.

nodes.label: Bollywood Music
nodes.summary: Film music from India's Hindi-language movie industry, blending Indian classical ragas, folk melodies, and Western pop/disco/electronic production. The world's largest music industry by output.

nodes.label: Flamenco
nodes.summary: Andalusian art form combining guitar, singing (cante), dance, and handclaps. Romani, Moorish, Jewish, and Spanish roots. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Paco de Lucia modernized it.

nodes.label: Fado
nodes.summary: Portuguese urban folk music expressing saudade — melancholic longing. Born in 1820s Lisbon from sailor songs, African rhythms, and Brazilian modinha. Amalia Rodrigues, Mariza. UNESCO heritage.
